sections.heading: Overview
sections.content: The management of penile squamous cell carcinoma is guided by a multidisciplinary approach, prioritizing organ-sparing techniques for early-stage disease to preserve penile function and quality of life, while ensuring oncologic control through surgery, radiation, and systemic therapies as indicated. Treatment philosophy emphasizes accurate staging, risk stratification, and tailored therapy based on tumor stage, histologic grade, lymphovascular invasion, and lymph node status. Curative intent is standard for localized and regional disease, with palliative care integrated for advanced or metastatic settings. The rarity of penile cancer necessitates referral to specialized centers with experience in this malignancy to optimize outcomes [MS-4, MS-19].

sections.heading: MDT Requirements
sections.content: Effective management of penile cancer requires a multidisciplinary team (MDT) including urologic surgeons, medical oncologists, radiation oncologists, pathologists, radiologists, and reconstructive surgeons. The NCCN guidelines are developed by a panel of experts from these disciplines, highlighting the importance of collaborative decision-making. Referral to high-volume centers is recommended to ensure access to specialized techniques such as dynamic sentinel node biopsy and organ-sparing surgeries [MS-1, MS-10].

sections.heading: Performance Status Guide
sections.content: Performance status, often assessed using the Eastern Cooperative Oncology Group (ECOG) scale, is a critical factor in determining eligibility for systemic therapy and aggressive treatments. Patients with ECOG performance status ≥1 are considered to have poor prognosis and may have limited tolerance for intensive regimens like TIP chemotherapy. In the metastatic setting, performance status helps guide the choice between curative-intent consolidation surgery or palliative systemic therapy and supportive care [MS-3, MS-17].

cards.title: Curative
cards.detail: Organ-sparing surgery (e.g., wide local excision, laser therapy, glansectomy) for low-risk lesions; partial or total penectomy for higher-risk or invasive tumors; dynamic sentinel node biopsy or inguinal lymph node dissection for nodal staging; radiation therapy as an alternative for penile preservation in select cases; adjuvant therapies (e.g., chemotherapy, chemoradiation) for high-risk features post-surgery.

cards.title: Palliative
cards.detail: Systemic chemotherapy (e.g., TIP, cisplatin/fluorouracil with pembrolizumab) as first-line; radiation therapy for local control or symptom relief; clinical trials for subsequent-line options; best supportive care for refractory disease.
